anchor.snippet: a von Neumann algebra or W*-algebra is a *-algebra of bounded operators
His double commutant theorem shows that the analytic definition is equivalent

annotations.label: Essentially bounded functions on the real line

anchor.snippet: of essentially bounded measurable functions on the real line is a commutative von Neumann algebra

annotations.label: All bounded operators on a Hilbert space

anchor.snippet: of all bounded operators on a Hilbert space
The first and most common way is to define them as weakly closed *-algebras of bounded operators
The *-algebras of bounded operators that are closed in the norm topology are C*-algebras
The second definition is that a von Neumann algebra is a subalgebra of the bounded operators closed under involution
The von Neumann double commutant theorem ( von Neumann 1930 ) says that the first two definitions are equivalent.
Sakai (1971) showed that von Neumann algebras can also be defined abstractly as C*-algebras that have a predual
The predual of a von Neumann algebra is in fact unique up to isomorphism.
A factor is a von Neumann algebra with trivial center
A finite von Neumann algebra is one which is the direct integral of finite factors
properly infinite von Neumann algebras are the direct integral of properly infinite factors
A von Neumann algebra that acts on a separable Hilbert space is called separable
The von Neumann algebra generated by a set of bounded operators on a Hilbert space is the smallest von Neumann algebra containing all those operators

annotations.label: Tensor product of von Neumann algebras

anchor.snippet: The tensor product of two von Neumann algebras acting on two Hilbert spaces is defined to be the von Neumann algebra generated by their algebraic tensor product
Von Neumann algebras are semihereditary : every finitely generated submodule of a projective module is itself projective

annotations.label: Affiliated operators form a von Neumann regular ring

anchor.snippet: The *-algebra of affiliated operators of a finite von Neumann algebra is a von Neumann regular ring

annotations.label: Classification of commutative von Neumann algebras

anchor.snippet: Every commutative von Neumann algebra is isomorphic to L ∞ ( X ) for some measure space
Operators E in a von Neumann algebra for which E = EE = E* are called projections
A subspace of the Hilbert space H is said to belong to the von Neumann algebra M if it is the image of some projection in M
This establishes a 1:1 correspondence between projections of M and subspaces that belong to M

annotations.label: Closures of images and kernels belong to M

anchor.snippet: It can be shown that the closure of the image of any operator in M and the kernel of any operator in M belongs to M
Two subspaces belonging to M are called ( Murray–von Neumann ) equivalent if there is a partial isometry mapping the first isomorphically onto the other
The equivalence relation ~ thus defined is additive in the following sense
The Schröder–Bernstein theorems for operator algebras gives a sufficient condition for Murray-von Neumann equivalence

annotations.label: Total order on equivalence classes in a factor

anchor.snippet: If M is a factor, ≤ is a total order on equivalence classes of projections
E is said to be a finite projection if there is no projection F < E

annotations.label: Von Neumann algebra generated by projections

anchor.snippet: a von Neumann algebra is generated by its projections; this is a consequence of the spectral theorem

annotations.label: Projections of a finite factor form a continuous geometry

anchor.snippet: The projections of a finite factor form a continuous geometry .
A von Neumann algebra N whose center consists only of multiples of the identity operator is called a factor
every von Neumann algebra on a separable Hilbert space is isomorphic to a direct integral of factors

annotations.label: Every factor has one of three types

anchor.snippet: Murray & von Neumann (1936) showed that every factor has one of 3 types as described below

annotations.label: Unique decomposition into types I, II, III

anchor.snippet: Every von Neumann algebra can be written uniquely as a sum of von Neumann algebras of types I, II, and III
A factor is called discrete (or occasionally tame ) if it has type I, and continuous
A factor is called semifinite if it has type I or II, and purely infinite if it has type III
A factor is called finite if the projection 1 is finite and properly infinite otherwise
A factor is said to be of type I if there is a minimal projection E ≠ 0
Any factor of type I is isomorphic to the von Neumann algebra of all bounded operators on some Hilbert space
A factor is said to be of type II if there are no minimal projections but there are non-zero finite projections
If the identity operator in a type II factor is finite, the factor is said to be of type II 1

annotations.label: Uniqueness of hyperfinite type II factors

anchor.snippet: These are the unique hyperfinite factors of types II 1 and II ∞

annotations.label: Unique tracial state on a type II_1 factor

anchor.snippet: Murray & von Neumann (1937) proved the fundamental result that a factor of type II 1 has a unique finite tracial state

annotations.label: Semifinite trace on type II_∞ factor

anchor.snippet: A factor of type II ∞ has a semifinite trace, unique up to rescaling

annotations.label: Fundamental group of a type II_∞ factor

anchor.snippet: is called the fundamental group of the type II ∞ factor
The tensor product of a factor of type II 1 and an infinite type I factor has type II ∞

annotations.label: Fundamental group of a type II_1 factor

anchor.snippet: The fundamental group of a type II 1 factor is defined to be the fundamental group of its tensor product

annotations.label: Connes: property (T) gives countable fundamental group

anchor.snippet: Connes then showed that the von Neumann group algebra of a countable discrete group with Kazhdan's property (T)
Sorin Popa showed that the fundamental group can be trivial for certain groups

annotations.label: Type II_1 group von Neumann algebra (ICC group)

anchor.snippet: An example of a type II 1 factor is the group von Neumann algebra of a countable infinite discrete group

annotations.label: McDuff: uncountably many type II_1 factors

anchor.snippet: McDuff (1969) found an uncountable family of such groups with non-isomorphic group von Neumann algebras
type III factors are factors that do not contain any nonzero finite projections at all
if the Connes spectrum (of its modular group) is 1 then the factor is of type III 0

annotations.label: Trace and projections on type III factors

anchor.snippet: The only trace on type III factors takes value ∞ on all non-zero positive elements
any type III factor can be written in a canonical way as the crossed product of a type II ∞ factor and the real numbers
Any von Neumann algebra M has a predual M ∗ , which is the Banach space of all ultraweakly continuous linear functionals on M

annotations.label: M is the dual of its predual

anchor.snippet: As the name suggests, M is (as a Banach space) the dual of its predual
The predual is unique in the sense that any other Banach space whose dual is M is canonically isomorphic to M ∗

annotations.label: Sakai: predual characterizes von Neumann algebras

anchor.snippet: Sakai (1971) showed that the existence of a predual characterizes von Neumann algebras among C* algebras

annotations.label: Predual is a proper closed subspace of the dual

anchor.snippet: The predual M ∗ is a closed subspace of the dual M* (which consists of all norm-continuous linear functionals on M ) but is generally smaller
The predual of the von Neumann algebra L ∞ ( R ) of essentially bounded functions on R is the Banach space L 1 ( R ) of integrable functions

annotations.label: Predual of B(H) is trace class operators

anchor.snippet: The predual of the von Neumann algebra B ( H ) of bounded operators on a Hilbert space H is the Banach space of all trace class operators

annotations.label: Predual of VN(G) is the Fourier algebra

anchor.snippet: The predual of the group von Neumann algebra VN ( G ) associated to the left regular representation of a locally compact group G is the Fourier algebra A ( G )
A weight ω on a von Neumann algebra is a linear map from the set of positive elements
A positive linear functional is a weight with ω(1) finite
A state is a weight with ω(1) = 1.
A trace is a weight with ω( aa* ) = ω( a*a ) for all a .
A tracial state is a trace with ω(1) = 1.
Any factor has a trace such that the trace of a non-zero projection is non-zero

annotations.label: Normal state from a unit vector (GNS)

anchor.snippet: then the functional a → ( av , v ) is a normal state
every such module H can be given an M -dimension
A module is called standard if it has a cyclic separating vector

annotations.label: Existence and uniqueness of standard representation

anchor.snippet: Each factor has a standard representation, which is unique up to isomorphism
Connes (1976) and others proved that the following conditions on a von Neumann algebra M on a separable Hilbert space H are all equivalent
The amenable factors have been classified: there is a unique one of each of the types

annotations.label: Amenable factors via group-measure space construction

anchor.snippet: All amenable factors can be constructed using the group-measure space construction of Murray and von Neumann

anchor.section: Tensor products of von Neumann algebras
anchor.snippet: The tensor product of two finite algebras is finite, and the tensor product of an infinite algebra and a non-zero algebra is infinite

annotations.label: Type of a tensor product is the maximum

anchor.section: Tensor products of von Neumann algebras
anchor.snippet: The type of the tensor product of two von Neumann algebras (I, II, or III) is the maximum of their types

anchor.section: Tensor products of von Neumann algebras
anchor.snippet: The commutation theorem for tensor products states that

anchor.section: Tensor products of von Neumann algebras
anchor.snippet: von Neumann (1938) showed that one should choose a state on each of the von Neumann algebras

anchor.section: Tensor products of von Neumann algebras
anchor.snippet: Araki & Woods (1968) studied the case where all the factors are finite matrix algebras

anchor.section: Tensor products of von Neumann algebras
anchor.snippet: Powers (1967) found an uncountable family of non-isomorphic hyperfinite type III λ factors for 0 < λ < 1, called Powers factors

anchor.section: Tensor products of von Neumann algebras
anchor.snippet: All hyperfinite von Neumann algebras not of type III 0 are isomorphic to Araki–Woods factors
A bimodule (or correspondence) is a Hilbert space H with module actions of two commuting von Neumann algebras
Any bimodule over two factors always gives a subfactor since one of the factors is always contained in the commutant of the other

annotations.label: Voiculescu: disjointness of non-amenable factor classes

anchor.snippet: Voiculescu has shown that the class of non-amenable factors coming from the group-measure space construction is disjoint from the class coming from group von Neumann algebras of free groups

annotations.label: Ozawa: hyperbolic groups give prime II_1 factors

anchor.snippet: Narutaka Ozawa proved that group von Neumann algebras of hyperbolic groups yield prime type II 1 factors

annotations.label: Essentially bounded functions on a σ-finite measure space

anchor.snippet: The essentially bounded functions on a σ-finite measure space form a commutative (type I 1 ) von Neumann algebra acting on the L 2 functions
A fundamental approximation theorem can be represented by the Kaplansky density theorem .

annotations.label: Bounded operators on a Hilbert space (type I factor)

anchor.snippet: The bounded operators on any Hilbert space form a von Neumann algebra, indeed a factor, of type I

annotations.label: Commutant of a unitary group representation

anchor.snippet: the bounded operators commuting with G form a von Neumann algebra G ′

annotations.label: Von Neumann group algebra of a discrete group

anchor.snippet: The von Neumann group algebra of a discrete group G is the algebra of all bounded operators

annotations.label: Tensor product as a von Neumann algebra

anchor.snippet: The tensor product of two von Neumann algebras, or of a countable number with states, is a von Neumann algebra
The crossed product of a von Neumann algebra by a discrete (or more generally locally compact) group can be defined, and is a von Neumann algebra

annotations.label: Von Neumann algebras of equivalence relations and groupoids

anchor.snippet: The von Neumann algebras of a measurable equivalence relation and a measurable groupoid can be defined
